issues.title: PresenceBar avatars lack keyboard focus, ARIA roles, and screen reader announcements
issues.description: Avatar circles in the presence bar are not focusable via keyboard, have no role='list'/role='listitem' structure, and presence changes are not announced via aria-live. Tooltips only appear on hover, not on focus. Violates WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard and screen reader requirements from Global Acceptance Standards.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/PresenceBar.tsx, make these specific changes to the component that renders the list of avatar circles: BEFORE: The avatar container is a plain div with no ARIA attributes, individual avatars are divs without role or tabIndex, and there is no aria-live region announcing presence changes. Tooltips only trigger on hover. AFTER: (1) Wrap the avatar list in a div with role='list' and aria-label='People currently viewing this page'. (2) Wrap each avatar div in a role='listitem'. (3) Make each avatar div focusable with tabIndex={0} and add aria-label={`${user.displayName}, ${statusLabel}`} where statusLabel is 'active'/'idle'/'away'. (4) Ensure Radix Tooltip opens on focus (add delayDuration={0} on Tooltip.Root if not present, this is default Radix behavior but verify the Trigger wraps a focusable element). (5) Add an aria-live='polite' visually hidden div at the end of the component that updates its text content when the number of viewers changes, e.g. '3 people viewing this page'. Use a useEffect that watches the others count and updates a state string. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EditConflictGuard useOptimisticLock silently fails when supabase query returns unexpected table name
issues.description: The useOptimisticLock hook casts supabase.from(table as any) which suppresses type checking. If an invalid table name is passed, the query fails silently and checkFailed is set but the consuming component may not handle it. The toast fires but there is no loading state during the conflict check, and the retry logic only retries once with no exponential backoff.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EditConflictGuard.tsx, in the useOptimisticLock hook's checkConflict callback, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The function casts supabase.from(table as any), has no isChecking loading state, retries only once with a hardcoded 1000ms delay, and the checkFailed state is only set inside checkConflict but never reset when a new entityId is provided. AFTER: (1) Add an isChecking boolean state, set it true at the start of checkConflict and false in a finally block. Return isChecking from the hook so consuming components can show a spinner. (2) Add a useEffect that resets conflictDetected, serverUpdatedAt, and checkFailed to their initial values when entityId changes, preventing stale conflict state from a previous entity. (3) Validate the table parameter at the top of checkConflict: if table is empty or entityId is empty, return false immediately without querying. (4) Keep the retry at 1 attempt but add a console.warn logging the table name and error message on failure so debugging is possible. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EntityPresence overflow button and PresenceDots have sub-44px visual targets despite 44px wrappers
issues.description: The PresenceDots component wraps each 24px avatar circle in a 44px button for touch targets, which is correct. However the overflow count indicator (+N) is also inside a 44px button but the visible circle is only 20px and placed inside a flex center, making it hard to visually identify as tappable on mobile. More critically, there is no empty/loading state: when presenceStore is not initialized, the component returns null, which causes layout shift when presence data arrives.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EntityPresence.tsx, in the PresenceDots component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: When isInitialized is false, the component returns null causing layout shift. The overflow button renders a tiny circle that is visually unclear on mobile. There is no skeleton or placeholder. AFTER: (1) When isInitialized is false OR viewers.length is 0, render a placeholder div with the same height (24px) and minWidth (24px) as the avatar row but with visibility:'hidden' to reserve space and prevent layout shift. (2) For the overflow button (+N), increase the visible circle size from 20px to 24px to match the avatar circles, and set the fontSize to typography.fontSize.caption so the number is readable. (3) Add aria-live='polite' to the container div so screen readers announce when viewers join or leave. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: PresenceBar does not handle Liveblocks connection errors or disconnected state
issues.description: The PresenceBar uses useOthers from Liveblocks but has no error boundary or fallback for when the Liveblocks connection fails (network issues, room not found, auth expired). If useOthers throws or returns undefined during connection loss, the component crashes. There is no visual indicator showing connection status (connected/reconnecting/disconnected), which violates the Global Acceptance Standards requirement for real-time status indicators.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/PresenceBar.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The component calls useOthers() without any try-catch wrapper or connection status handling. If Liveblocks is disconnected or the hook returns an unexpected value, the component may crash or render stale data with no indicator. AFTER: (1) Wrap the useOthers() call result in a null-safe check: const others = useOthers() ?? []. This prevents crashes if the hook returns null/undefined during disconnection. (2) Add a small connection status dot next to the presence bar: import useStatus from '../../lib/liveblocks' (if available) or check if others is an empty array when it previously had items to detect disconnection. If useStatus is not available in the Liveblocks setup, skip this and instead add a try-catch in the useEffect that populates the presenceStore, catching errors and logging them with console.warn rather than crashing. (3) When the others array is empty and was previously non-empty within the last 5 seconds, show a subtle 'Reconnecting...' text with a spinning RefreshCw icon (already imported) instead of hiding the bar entirely. Use a useRef to track the previous others length and a setTimeout. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EditConflictGuard missing conflict resolution UI for merge/overwrite choice
issues.description: When a conflict is detected (another user updated the entity since it was loaded), the component shows a banner but per the PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA Global Standards, edit lock conflicts should show the other user's avatar and name ('Walker is currently editing this item') and offer clear resolution: 'Reload to see their changes' or 'Overwrite with your version'. Currently the conflict banner exists but the dismissConflict function just hides it without actually resolving the data conflict, which could lead to silent data loss.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EditConflictGuard.tsx, in the conflict banner rendering section (the JSX that displays when conflictDetected is true), make these specific changes: BEFORE: The conflict banner shows a generic warning with a dismiss X button. Dismissing simply sets conflictDetected to false via dismissConflict(), which hides the warning but leaves the user editing stale data. There is no 'Reload' or 'Force Save' option. AFTER: (1) Replace the single dismiss X button with two explicit action buttons: a primary 'Reload Latest' button (uses RefreshCw icon, already imported) that calls window.location.reload() or a provided onReload callback prop, and a secondary 'Keep My Edits' button (uses the existing dismiss behavior but with clearer labeling). (2) Add descriptive text: 'This item was updated by another user at {formatted serverUpdatedAt time}. Your unsaved changes may conflict.' Format the time using a simple relative time format (e.g., '2 minutes ago'). (3) Change the banner background from a generic warning color to colors.statusError at 10% opacity with a left border of 3px solid colors.statusError to match the design system's danger pattern. (4) Add role='alert' and aria-live='assertive' to the banner container so screen readers immediately announce the conflict. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore has basic presence indicators (who's online) but no edit conflict detection or real-time collaborative editing. Fieldwire has no presence features at all. Autodesk BIM 360 shows who's viewing a model but not who's editing documents. SiteSync's EditConflictGuard with optimistic locking is a genuine differentiator, but it needs the conflict resolution UX polished and the accessibility gaps closed to be production ready. The PresenceBar with Liveblocks integration is ahead of all competitors if the disconnection handling and mobile responsiveness are fixed.
summary: The Collaboration & Team module has promising foundations: Liveblocks-powered presence tracking, optimistic lock conflict detection, and entity-level presence indicators. However, it scores 41 overall due to five critical gaps. Accessibility is the worst offender: presence avatars lack keyboard navigation, ARIA roles, and screen reader announcements, violating WCAG 2.1 AA requirements. The EditConflictGuard detects conflicts but offers no meaningful resolution UI (reload vs. overwrite), risking silent data loss. The PresenceBar has no disconnection handling, so network drops cause crashes or stale state with no user feedback. Mobile responsiveness is functional but has layout shift issues when presence data loads. TypeScript quality is acceptable but the `supabase.from(table as any)` cast in the lock hook suppresses safety. Fixing these five issues would raise the score to approximately 60 and make the collaboration layer production-viable.
